# 📜 Project Requirement

# Serious Game \- PIR

Consolidated Project Requirements & Direction (Post-Meeting)

## 1\. Project Philosophy (very important)

This project is not about building a finished business simulation.

It is about:

* Creating a simple but solid foundation  
* Designing a scalable architecture  
* Leaving behind a clean, well-documented base for future students

There are two deliverables:

1. What we build this semester  
2. What future teams will be able to extend

Learning objectives for students:

* Needs analysis  
* Role-based systems  
* Web application architecture  
* Clean, scalable design  
* Documentation and handover

## 2\. Needs Analysis

A proper needs analysis must be done before coding.

### Stakeholders

* Students are the  players  
* Teachers are the game masters

### Outputs

* Player needs (clarity, feedback, competition, simplicity)  
* Game master needs (control, visibility, reusability, class management)

This will justify every later design decision.

## 3\. Roles & Access

### Roles

* Player / Student  
* Game Master / Teacher

### Core Features

* Login system  
* Role-based access

Each game instance has:

* One game owner / game master  
* Multiple teams of players

## 4\. Game Structure

### Multiplayer World

* One shared world / market  
* Multiple teams  
* Actions from one team affect:  
  * The world  
  * Other teams

### Simplicity First

* The game can be extremely simple  
* It does not need to be business-related  
* Interest and clarity matter more than realism

### Rounds

* The game runs in rounds  
* Each round may behave slightly differently  
* Decisions → simulation → results → next round

### Variables

* Start with 2–3 global variables  
* These variables affect all teams  
* Architecture must allow adding more later

## 5\. Modes & Progression

* Possible game modes:  
  * Easy  
  * Hard  
* Same core engine, different parameters

Future teams should be able to:

* Add complexity  
* Add variables  
* Add mechanics

## 6\. Game Management Features

* Save a game in progress  
* Resume later  
* Teachers can run the game:  
  * Across multiple classes  
  * Over a semester  
* Game master can:  
  * Rename the game  
  * Control progression

(Kahoot-style ownership model)

## 7\. Interface & UX

### UI Requirements

* Modern  
* Clean  
* Minimalist  
* No AI-generated images  
* No “AI website builders”

### Language

* English

### Visual Identity

* Cool name for the game  
* Simple decorative elements  
* Consistent style

The UI must be agreed upon before implementation.

## 8\. Technical Constraints

### Platform

* Web-based  
* Accessible on and off campus  
* No VPN  
* Hosted by INSA

### Infrastructure

* Meet with CSN (IT services):  
  * Hosting/Sub-domain :   
  * Database :   
  * Certificates :   
  * Security constraints :   
  * Authentication : 

Réservé aux gens de l’INSA donc VPN

Authentification via un module CAS pour avoir accès 

Mise à disposition d’un serveur virtuel avec base de données avec postgresql donc nécessite la liste des personnes qui doivent se connecter. Serveur vierges avec nombre de services à déployer genre postgresql. 

Gitlab enseignement.  

Accès filtré. BDD accessible que depuis le serveur. Mise à jour de sécurité à laisser. 

Choisir environnement prod.test ou just prod 

Montée de versions à prévoir pour les prochaines équipes.

## 9\. Scalability & Code Quality

Whatever stack you choose:

* Must be scalable  
* Must be extensible  
* Must be understandable by future students

### Mandatory

* Clean code  
* Clear architecture  
* Good documentation  
* Explicit extension points

Future teams should be able to:

* Add variables  
* Add rules  
* Increase complexity  
* Approach business-game-level simulation over time

## 10\. Supervision & Project Governance

* Paul and Oumaima are the main technical & conceptual supervisors  
* Focus is on execution, not academic politics  
* Details will be reviewed in LV1  
* Regular updates expected  
* Team proposes → supervisors validate → team builds

# Rugby Team Management

### Refined & PIR-Compatible Version

## 1\. High-level concept

A multiplayer rugby team management simulation where 4 teams compete in a shared league over 6 rounds. Each team makes strategic decisions (player recruitment, marketing investment) that affect match outcomes, finances, and popularity. The game runs in rounds, includes controlled randomness, and is designed to be simple, extensible, and reusable by teachers across semesters.

## 2\. Fixed structure

These are non-negotiable constants:

* 4 teams  
* 6 cycles / rounds  
* All teams start with:  
  * Same money  
  * Same number of players  
  * Same initial stats

## 3\. Roles & access

### Roles

* Player / Student  
* Admin / Game Master (Teacher)

### Admin capabilities

* Set:  
  * Starting money  
  * Initial players  
* Launch / pause / resume a game  
* Own the game instance

Important:  
Admin does not micro-manage gameplay during rounds. They configure, observe, and reuse.

## 4\. Core game loop

Each round follows exactly the same structure:

### Phase 1 — Decisions

Each team chooses:

1. Recruitment  
   * Buy players from the market  
   * simple auction  
2. Marketing  
   * Invest money to increase fan growth probability

That’s it. Two decisions only.

### Phase 2 — Simulation

The engine computes:

* Match results  
* Fan evolution  
* Money gained  
* Random events

### Phase 3 — Results

* Match scores  
* League table (points)  
* Updated money & fans  
* Injuries / incidents

Then, we go to the next round.

## 5\. Matches & tournament logic

### Tournament

* Each round \= matches between teams  
* Scoring:  
  * Win → 4 points  
  * Draw → 2 points  
  * Loss → 0 points  
* After 6 rounds → winner \= most points

### Match probability

Depends on:

* Team strength (sum or average of player scores)  
* Luck factor (randomness)

## 6\. Players & recruitment

* Players have:  
  * Position  
  * One global score (skill)  
  * Price  
* Each round:  
  * A small set of new players enters the market  
* Simple auction (highest bid wins)

### Later (explicitly “for future teams”)

* Player cards  
* Multiple attributes  
* Advanced auctions  
* Draft systems

## 7\. Fans, money & marketing

### Fans

* Each team has a fan count  
* Fans generate money each round (merch sales)

### Marketing decision

* Teams can spend money on advertising  
* This increases probability of:  
  * Gaining fans next round

### Sporting results

* Win → chance to gain fans  
* Loss → chance to lose fans

This creates a feedback loop:

Results → fans → money → better players → results

That’s exactly what they want.

## 8\. Global risks & randomness

### Risks (limit to 1–2)

* Injury: player unavailable next round  
* Scandal (anti-doping): temporary stat penalty

These are probabilistic events, not deep systems.

Add them as:

* Simple random checks  
* Clearly logged in results

## 9\. Inter-team interaction

Your idea already satisfies this without direct PvP actions:

* All teams share:  
  * The same market  
  * The same fan attention  
  * The same league table

This is good because:

* No real-time interaction  
* No chat  
* No coordination complexity

## 10\. Variables

For validation, say clearly:

Initial global variables:

1. Luck factor (match randomness)  
2. Fan growth sensitivity  
3. Injury probability

## 11\. Exclusions

You should explicitly state:

* No real financial accounting  
* No complex player attributes  
* No real-time gameplay  
* No AI-generated assets  
* No business simulation depth

Rugby teams :  

🎮 **Serious Game Concept – Rugby Team Management**

The game simulates the strategic management of a rugby team over the course of a **6-match tournament**, combining **sporting, economic, and marketing management**, with elements of **risk and randomness**.

🏉 **Team and Player Management**

Players have a **card** and an **overall score** representing their skill level.

Teams can **buy players**:

* through an **auction system** (the highest bidder wins),

At each cycle, **3 new players per position** are added to the market.

Managers can either **freely choose their purchases** or **select from 3 proposed player cards**.

⚽ **Matches and Tournament**

Teams compete in a tournament:

* Win: **4 points**

* Draw: **2 points**

* Loss: **0 points**

After 6 matches, the team with the highest number of points wins the tournament.

The probability of winning depends on:

* the **combined statistics of the team’s players**,

* a **luck factor**.

Random risks are included:

* **player injury** during a match,

* **positive anti-doping test** for a top player.


👥 **Fans and Popularity**

Each team has a **fan count**.

Fans generate **money each round** through **merchandise sales**.

Teams can:

* **invest in advertising** to increase the probability of gaining new fans.

Sporting results impact popularity:

* a win can increase the number of fans,

* a loss can result in losing fans.

The teacher will be able to choose the amount of money or the team at the start and the players. It means that in a login part you can choose to be “admin”.

4 teams 

6 cycles 

They start with equal money and equal players with equal score or stats.

After that they can buy some players, (*invest in their cards)*

About the investment part we can do a 3 card choice or they can do what they want. 

At each cycle we should add something like 3 players for each place in the team. 

This is a tournament so if you win you get 4 points, draw is 2 points and losing gets you 0 points. At the end of the 6 matches the team who gets more points is winning. 

Buy players  
Buy advertisement for a probability to increase the number of fans

Fan count converts to money at each round by Selling merch to the fans

Fans count 

Players got a general score, number that represents its skill, this number will be used on the match part for the probability of winning the match

	When you win a match you can win some fans.   
	If you lose probability of losing fans

Add risk of player injury during the match   
Add risk of very good player getting caught on anti doping

Inter-team connexion : 

If you are winning \+ you are selling \+  merch with good quality, you are getting more chances of getting new fans. On the other hand you can lose some fans.

The stats of all the players are combined together and put against the other team during the football match.

In these two formulas we should add some luck factor.  

## Decisions

the team will deliver a full playable 6-round experience this semester, while still putting strong architecture and documentation in place for future cohorts. You’ll keep a clear MVP boundary but allow 2–3 extra features if time permits. When stakeholder requirements conflict, the team lead will decide after documenting tradeoffs in a requirements matrix, and you’ll run in 2-week sprints with client validation every two weeks (with twice-weekly meetings). The platform will support multiple concurrent games per teacher (realistically a few per semester), with permanent ownership by the creating teacher. The game will be persistently saved “on every change” (decisions/bids/state updates). Team switching is teacher-controlled, while the initial team formation is still to be validated with Paul (auto-assign only if explicitly required). Round orchestration will be timer-based with teacher override, and teams can edit decisions until the round closes. For gameplay, recruitment will use a simple auction model, new players appear per position each round, players remain minimal (position \+ score \+ price), matches follow a hidden predefined round-robin schedule, and randomness is seeded per game/round for reproducibility. Risks target injury \+ scandal if time allows (otherwise drop them), and core global variables are luck factor, fan growth sensitivity, and injury probability. Results will show league table, money, fans, plus a recap of each team’s decisions, while formulas remain hidden to players. UX will be English-first then French later; you want wireframes approved before coding but also accept quick build-and-refine and minimal polish in V1, and game identity is configurable per instance by the teacher. Technically, you’ll separate “platform engine” from “game rules,” use a database, keep API style TBD, use demo-mode auth if CSN/SSO is uncertain, infer roles from SSO groups/attributes when integrated, develop with main+feature branches, require code review (but not necessarily tests/docs per feature), and run lightweight daily check-ins with weekly review.

## Questions for stakeholders

### A) Team formation

1. If Paul doesn’t decide in time, what’s our default?  
    A. Teacher manually assigns students to teams (admin UI)  
    B. Students join via team code (teacher distributes codes)  
    C. Auto-assign evenly at first login

2. How do students enter a specific game instance?  
    A. Game code (like Kahoot) \+ join link

### B) “Save on every change” vs performance/complexity

3. What does “save on every change” mean in practice?  
    A. Save on every user action (each bid edit, each decision edit)  
    B. Save on important transitions only (submit decision, close round, simulate, publish)  
    C. Save on debounce (e.g., every 5–10 seconds while editing) \+ on submit

### C) Auction design details (must lock for implementation)

4. Auction type  
    A. Sealed-bid per round (everyone submits bids, highest wins at close)  
    B. Timed live auction during the round (more UI complexity)  
    C. Hybrid (not recommended for V1)

5. Tie-breaking rule (same bid)  
    A. Earliest bid wins  
    B. Random but seeded  
    C. Higher current league rank loses tie (anti-snowball)

6. Budget rule  
    A. Must have money available at bid time (no overbidding)  
    B. Allow overbidding but penalize later (riskier)  
    C. Allow but reject if wins and can’t pay (messy)

### D) Round timing and deadlines

7. Default round duration  
    A. Fixed duration (e.g., 10–15 minutes)  
    B. Teacher chooses duration per round  
    C. No timer by default; teacher manual only

8. Late submissions  
    A. Default decisions applied automatically at deadline  
    B. Teacher decides case-by-case (override)  
    C. Penalize \+ apply defaults

### E) Randomness \+ transparency

9. Do we expose “seeded randomness” to anyone?  
    A. Hidden entirely  
    B. Teacher-only debug view (seed \+ roll log)  
    C. Visible to all (usually unnecessary)

### F) API style (you marked TBD—needs decision early)

10. API/UI architecture  
     A. Server-rendered MVC (fastest, simplest)  
     B. REST API \+ SPA frontend (more work, cleaner separation)  
     C. Hybrid (server pages \+ a few API endpoints for async)

### G) Definition of Done (you chose “code \+ review only”—confirm tradeoff)

11. Do we require minimal tests for core engine \+ state machine anyway?  
     A. Yes, mandatory for engine \+ round orchestration only  
     B. Yes, mandatory for everything (heavier)  
     C. No tests required (high risk for fairness bugs)

### Default

If we want to exit requirements and enter design, we should lock defaults now.

My *recommended defaults* (with rationale) are:

1. Team formation: Teacher manually assigns (fallback)  
    Because it’s simplest, avoids edge cases, and works even if SSO roster/group mapping isn’t ready.

2. Join a game instance: Game code \+ join link (Kahoot style)  
    Matches the “ownership model” and keeps class operations simple.

3. Saving: Save on important transitions only (submit/close/simulate/publish)  
    “Save on every change” is nice, but it increases complexity and concurrency risk fast.

4. Auction model: Sealed-bid per round  
    Avoids real-time UI complexity and still feels like an auction.

5. Tie-break: Seeded random (but logged)  
    Fair, simple, and consistent with reproducibility.

6. Budget rule: Must have money available at bid time  
    Prevents messy “can’t pay after winning” states.

7. Round timing: Teacher chooses duration with a default (e.g., 10–15 min)  
    So it works for both a short in-class session and longer homework-style rounds.

8. Late submissions: Auto-default decision at deadline \+ teacher override  
    Keeps the state machine moving and avoids blocking the whole class.

9. Seed visibility: Teacher-only debug view  
    Players don’t need it; teacher may need it to justify fairness or debug.

10. Architecture: Hybrid (server-rendered pages \+ a few async endpoints)  
     Fastest path to a clean platform without building a whole SPA.

11. Testing requirement: Mandatory tests for engine \+ round orchestration only  
     That’s where fairness/consistency bugs would kill trust.

# 🖼️ Framework & Umbrella Activities

# Why do we need this?

In our case, the need for change comes from a clear operational and pedagogical business need around an existing legacy teaching tool. INSA currently uses an older business simulation game to teach first-year students how decisions affect results and how to interpret financial documents (e.g., profit, stock, cash/treasury, balance sheet logic). However, the current solution does not scale well and is difficult to operate in a modern teaching context: access is limited, onboarding is not smooth, and the tool is not designed for large cohorts where every student can connect, authenticate, and participate directly. This creates the need to adapt a legacy system to a changing environment (larger classes, web access expectations, institutional hosting/security constraints) and to extend the usable features around it (role-based access for teachers, multi-game instances, save/resume over multiple sessions). Because we cannot realistically modify the existing proprietary software, the project becomes the creation of a new system that preserves the same educational loop (decisions, simulation, results, analysis) while providing a clean, scalable, INSA-hosted foundation that future student teams can extend.

# Framework Activities

## Understand the Problem 

### Who has a stake in the solution (stakeholders)?

The direct stakeholders are: 

- the **students**, who will use the platform as players  
- the **teachers**, who will act as game masters: they need to run sessions, observe progress, reuse the activity across cohorts, and keep control of game instances over time.  
- **INSA as an institution** is also a stakeholder because the platform supports INSA’s teaching mission and needs to remain usable across years (continuity, maintainability, deployment on school infrastructure, and alignment with institutional constraints).  
- **CSN (INSA IT/infrastructure/security team)** is a critical stakeholder because they govern hosting conditions, certificates/domains, security constraints, and any integration with INSA authentication and identity services.  
- **Future student teams** are stakeholders because the deliverable is explicitly a V1 foundation that must be extendable; their needs drive clean architecture, documentation, and explicit extension points.   
- The **PIR supervisors and evaluators** (including the English/report side) are also stakeholders because grading criteria shape what must be demonstrated (needs analysis quality, role-based system, architecture clarity, documentation/handover).  
- institutional stakeholders around **compliance and data governance** (e.g., what user data is stored, retention expectations, and what is acceptable to log), even if those rules are enforced through CSN rather than by us directly.

### What are the unknowns?

The unknowns fall into three categories: institutional constraints, product requirements, and simulation/game design constraints.

On the institutional side, authentication is an unknown but in a very specific way: we do not yet know how INSA identity integration will work technically (SSO protocol, what data we receive back, whether we can map roles from groups/attributes, how we distinguish teacher vs student accounts, what session mechanism we must use, etc.). We also do not know the final hosting constraints (deployment model, database availability, domain/certificates process, operational constraints). 

Importantly, there may be **requirement tensions** across stakeholders: one client wants “no VPN” for accessibility while another prioritizes “security/efficiency first.” Whether access is possible off campus without VPN is a CSN decision, but the *conflict itself* is our unknown: we must document it, surface it early, and design the platform so that it can still be demonstrated even if the final auth/network integration is not ready (e.g., a prototype mode with “teacher / student” selection while the rest of the product is validated).

On the product side, there are major unknowns beyond login: how teachers want to operationalize games across classes and time (one game per class vs multiple parallel games; templates; ownership model; rename/reuse/archive; how “save/resume” is expected to work across weeks). We also need clarity on the teacher’s desired level of control over progression (strict round deadlines vs manual advance; can teams edit decisions; what happens if a team doesn’t submit). Team management is also unknown: whether teams should be **teacher-defined** (most likely), student self-join via a code.

On the simulation side, the unknowns are what makes the game credible and teachable: how much randomness is acceptable, whether outcomes must be reproducible (seeded randomness) for fairness and debugging, what “minimum results” teachers need to run a debrief (league table, money/fans evolution, incidents, decision recap), and how we prevent obvious abuse (late submissions, duplicate submissions, inconsistent states). These determine the rules engine requirements more than any UI detail.

### What data, functions, and features are required to properly solve the problem?

At the data level, we need: 

- users with roles  
- game instances with an owner/game master;  
- teams and membership;  
- round states;   
- submitted decisions;   
- simulation outputs (results);   
- events/incidents;   
- persistent storage to support saving and resuming a game later.   
- We also need a structured way to record “what happened” each round (a minimal audit/history) so results are explainable and debuggable—this is essential for teaching and for future extensions.

Functionally, the minimum system must support: 

- role-based access (teacher vs student),   
- creation and management of game instances (ownership model, rename, configure initial parameters),   
- team management (likely teacher-defined assignment and membership),   
- a round workflow (open round → collect decisions → simulate → publish results → advance),   
- clear results views (ranking plus key indicators and incidents).   
- A bilingual interface and consistent minimalist UI are part of the requirements because the tool is meant for repeated classroom use.   
- Explicit extension points are not “nice to have”: they are required because the deliverable is a V1 foundation that future cohorts will expand with new variables, mechanics, and potentially new game themes.

### Can the problem be compartmentalized?

Yes, and we should do it deliberately to reduce risk and keep planning realistic. The project splits into: 

- Identity/auth integration (with a prototype fallback),   
- authorization (role checks),   
- game instance lifecycle (create/configure/save/resume/archive)  
- team management (creation, membership, joining/assignment rules)  
- round orchestration (state machine \+ deadlines/locks)  
- simulation engine (rules \+ randomness \+ reproducibility strategy)  
- persistence and history (data model \+ audit)  
- UI/UX (student vs teacher flows, bilingual switch, results clarity). 

This compartmentalization matters because some modules depend on CSN decisions (auth/hosting), while others can be built and validated immediately (game loop, results, persistence, UX).

### Can the problem be represented graphically?

Yes, and it will improve both planning and the report. We can represent: a **stakeholder map** including INSA and CSN, a **use-case diagram** for teacher vs student actions, a r**ound-state machine** (when decisions are allowed, when simulation runs, when results are published), and an **entity-relationship diagram** for core data (GameInstance, Team, User, Round, Decision, Result, Event, Membership). A simple “**end-to-end flow” diagram** (login/join → decide → submit → simulate → results → next round) is also useful because it aligns clients quickly without diving into implementation.

### Can an analysis model be created?

Yes. Our analysis model should capture 

1. the core domain entities and relationships (ownership, team membership, rounds, decisions, results)  
2. the process constraints (submission rules, locking, progression control, handling missing submissions)  
3. the system boundaries: what we implement versus what is provided or constrained by CSN (hosting, authentication, network/security policies). The value of this model is that it becomes the “contract” linking stakeholder needs to design decisions, and it remains valid even if one implementation detail (like final SSO integration) is delayed—because the game platform and its workflow can still be validated and extended.

## Plan a solution (modeling and software design).

Design a platform first, then plug a simple game into it. That means defining:

- Domain model (GameInstance, Round, Team, Player/User, Market/World state, Decisions, Results, Events)  
- Round workflow (Decisions → Simulation → Results → Next Round)  
- Access model (player vs game master)  
- Extension points explicitly: how to add new global variables, new decision types, new random events, or even swap the game scenario later.  
- Scalable web architecture, define APIs/pages, decide how state is stored (save/resume), and lock the UI direction (clean, minimalist, bilingual).

### Have we seen similar problems before?

Yes. Even if the game theme is different, the underlying problem is a classic one: a **multi-user web application** with **role-based access**, **shared state**, and a **structured workflow** (rounds). We have also seen similar patterns in classroom tools (Kahoot-style “game instance ownership”), online platforms where one facilitator controls progression, and turn-based games where users submit decisions and then receive results. SimGest itself is evidence that the pedagogical pattern works: fixed rounds, decision sheet, centralized simulation, result interpretation, repeat.

### Has a similar problem been solved? Are elements reusable?

Yes, at two levels. Conceptually, SimGest and other serious games show the reusable structure (decisions → simulation → results). Technically, many elements are standard and reusable through well-known approaches:

- authentication and session management,  
- RBAC (role-based access control),   
- REST APIs for submission and retrieval,   
- a database for persistence,  
- a state machine for the round lifecycle.

Even if we cannot reuse SimGest code, we can reuse proven software engineering solutions: common web app architecture patterns (MVC or layered architecture), ORM-backed data models, and standard i18n frameworks for French/English. We can also reuse reusable UI components (tables for rankings/results, forms for decisions, admin dashboards) while respecting the “no AI website builders” constraint.

### Can subproblems be defined? Are solutions apparent for them?

Yes — and planning depends on it. The project can be broken into subproblems with fairly standard solutions:

* **Game lifecycle**: create/configure game instance, ownership model, save/resume/archive.  
* **Round orchestration**: round states, submission windows, locking, progression controlled by teacher.  
* **Team management**: create teams, assign members (teacher-defined or join-by-code), enforce membership.  
* **Decision capture**: two-decision form per round, validation, prevention of duplicate/late submissions.  
* **Simulation engine**: deterministic core rules \+ controlled randomness (optionally seedable for fairness/debug).  
* **Results & analytics**: ranking table, key indicators (money/fans), events (injury/scandal), history per round.  
* **Security & deployment alignment**: integrate CSN constraints later without blocking core features (prototype fallback if SSO isn’t ready).  
  For each of these, solutions are readily apparent because they map to standard web application patterns and can be implemented incrementally.

### Are there recognizable patterns and existing software that implement required features?

Yes. Patterns include:

* **Role-based dashboards** (teacher vs student views).  
* **Turn-based workflow state machine** (open → collect → close → simulate → publish).  
* **Event logging / audit trail** (what happened each round, why results changed).  
* **Plugin-like extensibility** (rules/variables defined in a way that future teams can add without rewriting core).  
  And for implementation, we can rely on existing mature libraries/framework features rather than reinventing: RBAC middleware, i18n systems, database migrations, and UI component libraries. The key is to keep the “game content” isolated from the “platform engine,” so reuse and extension are natural.

### Can we represent a solution so it leads to effective implementation? Can a design model be created?

Yes. We can represent the solution with a small design package that directly maps to implementation tasks:

* A **module diagram** separating platform concerns (auth/roles, persistence, game lifecycle) from game concerns (rules engine, variables, events).  
* A **round state machine** that defines allowed actions and transitions.  
* An **ER diagram** for data persistence (GameInstance, Team, Membership, Round, Decision, Result, Event).  
* A set of **API contracts** (submit decision, view results, advance round, manage teams).  
* UI wireframes for the minimalist bilingual interface (student decision page, results page, teacher control panel).  
  This design model becomes the implementation roadmap: each diagram maps to components, endpoints, and tests, and it remains valid even if infrastructure choices (SSO details, hosting constraints) are finalized later with CSN.

## Carry out the plan (code generation). 

Building the minimum complete loop end-to-end: authentication \+ role checks, game creation/ownership, one round submission flow for teams, a simulation engine that consumes decisions and produces results, and a results dashboard (league table, money/fans updates, incidents). 

Then we add persistence so a game can be paused and resumed, and we make sure the app can be deployed on INSA infrastructure. The key is that the code is structured so later teams can add complexity without rewriting core components.

### Does the solution conform to the plan?

During implementation, we will continuously check that what we build matches the requirements and the design model agreed with the supervisors. Concretely, that means we implement the platform in the same layered structure we planned (roles/auth → game instance lifecycle → team management → round orchestration → simulation engine → results), and we avoid “feature creep” that would turn the V1 into a full business simulator. Each sprint/iteration should end with a demonstrable slice of the planned loop (decisions → simulation → results) so we can verify alignment early and often.

### Is source code traceable to the design model?

Yes, we will make traceability explicit. Each design element (entities, state machine transitions, APIs, UI views) will map to a corresponding code component and be documented. Examples: the ER diagram maps to database models/migrations; the round state machine maps to a single orchestration service that enforces allowed actions; use cases map to routes/controllers; and the simulation rules map to a dedicated “engine” module. We also keep a simple requirements-to-implementation trace: user stories or requirement IDs in the repo issues, referenced in pull requests and commit messages. This makes it easy for future teams to understand why each part exists and where to extend it.

### Is each component part of the solution provably correct?

For a web-based serious game, “provably correct” means we can formally specify and then verify critical invariants and behaviors. We can do this with a mix of strong validation and testing:

* **State invariants:** a round cannot be simulated before it is closed; results cannot be edited after publication; a team cannot submit twice; a user cannot act outside their role; a team cannot act in a game it does not belong to.  
* **Data invariants:** consistent membership, non-negative balances (if that’s a rule), player availability constraints (injury disables a player next round), and consistent point totals in the league table.  
* **Determinism controls:** if randomness is used, we can make it reproducible per game/round through seeding so outcomes can be debugged and fairness can be defended.  
  Each of these invariants can be tested automatically and also enforced at the service/database level (constraints, transactions) so correctness is not only “by convention.”

### Has the design and code been reviewed, or have correctness proofs been applied?

We will rely on disciplined engineering practices appropriate to the project scope. The baseline is systematic **code review via pull requests** (architecture consistency, readability, security basics, and extension readiness). For the parts that behave like algorithms—especially the round orchestration and simulation engine—we can go further by writing a small “proof-like” specification: a list of invariants and pre/post-conditions for each state transition and each simulation step. We then validate those specifications using tests (unit tests for the engine, property-based tests for invariants, integration tests for end-to-end workflows). This achieves the practical equivalent of correctness proofs in a software engineering context: the behavior is defined, reviewable, and automatically checked whenever the code changes.

## Examine result for accuracy (testing & quality assurance).

For our project, “accuracy” means the system behaves consistently and fairly according to the rules we define. We validate the engine with test cases (same inputs → same outputs, correct handling of random seeds if used, no invalid states like negative money if disallowed), and we test the round workflow (a team can’t submit twice, can’t act after the round closes, game master controls progression). 

We also test role security (players cannot access admin actions), persistence (save/resume doesn’t corrupt state), and basic load/robustness (multiple teams submitting around the same time). 

Finally, we check usability: the UI is clear, bilingual switching works, and the results are understandable enough for classroom use.

### Is it possible to test each component part of the solution?

Yes, because we deliberately compartmentalize the system. The simulation engine can be tested independently with fixed inputs and expected outputs. The round orchestration can be tested as a state machine (allowed transitions, forbidden actions, locking rules). Team management and role-based access can be tested through authorization rules (who can do what). Persistence can be tested by creating a game, progressing rounds, saving, reloading, and checking that state is identical. The UI can be tested at the level of “critical user journeys” (student submits decisions, teacher advances round, everyone sees results).

### Has a reasonable testing strategy been implemented?

A reasonable strategy for our scope is a layered test approach:

* **Unit tests** for core logic: simulation calculations, points table computation, fan/money updates, event triggers, and invariants (no double submissions, no actions after lock, etc.).  
* **Integration tests** for workflows: create game → assign teams → open round → submit decisions → close/simulate → publish results → next round → save/resume.  
* **Security/authorization tests**: students cannot access game-master endpoints; teams cannot submit for other teams; only the owner can progress the game.  
* **Regression tests** for persistence and reproducibility: same game state \+ same decisions (+ same seed if used) gives the same results after reload.  
  This is also compatible with future teams: tests become living documentation of rules and constraints.

### Does the solution produce results that conform to required data, functions, and features?

Validation here means the platform actually demonstrates the promised teaching loop and operational features: users join the correct game/team, decisions are collected within a round, the engine produces results that are coherent with inputs, results are published clearly (ranking \+ key indicators \+ incidents), and the game can progress for 6 rounds with state preserved across sessions. We also verify that “shared world” interaction exists: one team’s decisions affect the overall environment (market/player pool/league ranking), not just their own isolated scoreboard. Finally, the system must remain intentionally simple in V1 while still being extendable: adding a new variable or rule should not require rewriting the whole platform.

### Has the software been validated against all stakeholder requirements?

We validate against stakeholders by turning their needs into explicit acceptance checks:

* **Teachers/game masters:** can create and own a game instance, configure initial parameters, control progression, view results clearly, and reuse/suspend/resume a game across sessions.  
* **Students/players:** can join easily (SSO if available, otherwise prototype mode), identify their team, submit decisions without confusion, and receive clear feedback each round.  
* **INSA:** the result is maintainable, documented, and deployable on institutional infrastructure, with a handover-ready codebase.  
* **CSN:** deployment and authentication choices are compatible with their constraints; if integration is not finalized, the prototype still demonstrates the core product while keeping an integration path ready.  
  This closes the loop: the project is not only “working code,” but a verified system aligned with the real classroom use case and the long-term goal of extension by future cohorts.

# Umbrella Activities

## Software project tracking and control.

We will track tasks, priorities, and progress using GitHub’s Kanban-style project board (issues, milestones, sprint-like iterations) so both implementation and documentation deliverables stay visible and controllable.

## Risk management.

We will maintain a lightweight risk list (e.g., dependency on CSN decisions, uncertainty around SSO integration, scope creep, availability of “client validation”) and review it regularly to decide mitigations (prototype fallback modes, strict MVP boundaries, early demos).

## Software quality assurance

Quality will be supported through automated checks in GitHub (CI pipeline), basic test coverage for core logic, and consistent coding standards (linting/formatting where appropriate). The exact QA scope (coverage targets, tools) is currently in review.

## Technical reviews.

All significant changes will go through GitHub pull requests with peer review, focusing on architecture alignment, maintainability, and extension readiness. We may also schedule periodic short design reviews with supervisors when major decisions need validation (in review).

## Measurement.

We can track simple, non-invasive metrics such as completed issues per iteration, build/test pass rate, and basic code health indicators (test coverage, lint status). Which metrics we’ll standardize is currently in review.

## Software configuration management.

GitHub will be our configuration management backbone: version control, branching strategy, tagged releases, and change history. CI configuration and environment variables/secrets management will be aligned with CSN hosting constraints (in review).

## Reusability management.

Because the deliverable is a V1 foundation for future cohorts, we will explicitly design for reuse: modular separation between “platform engine” and “game rules,” clear extension points, and developer documentation for how to add variables/mechanics (details in review).

## Work product preparation and production.

Work products include the codebase, deployment notes, technical documentation, and the PIR report deliverables (including analysis/design artifacts). Format and exact handover package structure are currently in review with supervisor expectations.

# 🌊 Process Flow

# Agile process flow

There are many different process flows possible but for our project (5 students, course context, real institutional constraints), agile is the best single choice because it is the only flow that directly matches all three realities written in our PIR doc:

1. We have big unknowns that will change mid-project:  
   1. We *don’t yet know* how INSA identity integration will work (SSO protocol, role mapping, session rules)  
   2. We *don’t know* the final hosting constraints (DB, domains/certificates, operational limits).  
   3. We also explicitly require a prototype fallback mode so we can still demonstrate the system if SSO isn’t ready. That is exactly an evolutionary mindset of agile: deliver a working increment now, swap/upgrade integration later without blocking the core platform.  
2. We must validate the “teaching loop” early, not at the end. Our own implementation plan says: *build the minimum complete loop end-to-end* (login/roles → team assignment → round submission → simulation → results), and each iteration ends with a demonstrable slice of “decisions → simulation → results” to verify alignment early and often. That is literally evolutionary delivery.  
3. The deliverable is a V1 foundation for future cohorts so our requirements emphasize a “simple but solid foundation,” “clean architecture,” “explicit extension points,” plus documentation/handover for future teams. Evolutionary fits because you can freeze stable increments (with tests \+ docs) that future teams can build on safely, instead of a single “big bang” handover.

## Why Scrum is better than *every other option* for this project

### 1\) Linear process flow / Waterfall

Waterfall assumes you can lock requirements early and test late. Your PIR says requirements and constraints (SSO \+ hosting \+ teacher workflow expectations) are not known yet and must be surfaced early. So waterfall maximizes rework risk and delays the moment you discover “we built the wrong thing.”

### 2\) Prototyping process model

We do need early validation, but our deliverable is not “a throwaway prototype.” It must be a maintainable V1 foundation for future cohorts, with clear extension points and handover-quality docs. Pure prototyping has a known failure mode you even listed: shipping a prototype or losing work in throwaway iterations. Scrum avoids that by making each increment “real” and cumulative.

### 3\) Spiral process model

Spiral is risk-analysis-heavy and best for large, complex, high-budget systems. Our own slides say failures in risk analysis can doom it and it requires an expert team.  We *do* have risks (CSN dependency, SSO uncertainty), and our PIR plans a lightweight risk list already  but Spiral would add management overhead you don’t have time/experience for in a 5-student class project.

### 4\) Unified Process

UP/RUP emphasizes documentation and overlapping phases and can be tricky to integrate. Our PIR wants documentation, but also emphasizes execution, MVP boundaries, and incremental build of the core loop. Scrum gives you the cadence and feedback loop without the heavyweight UP machinery.

### 5\) XP

XP expects high discipline (pair programming, constant tests, frequent meetings). With 5 students, mixed schedules, and class deadlines, XP as the *primary* method is risky.  
We can still borrow XP practices (unit tests for simulation/state invariants) inside Scrum, which our PIR already plans.

### 6)Kanban

Our PIR already plans a Kanban-style GitHub board , but Kanban alone doesn’t force:

* sprint goals,  
* timeboxed delivery,  
* predictable demo/review moments with supervisors.

In a course setting with “regular updates expected,” Scrum’s sprint cadence is safer.

### 7\) DevOps

DevOps practices (CI/CD, monitoring mindset) are great, and your PIR mentions CI and deployment alignment , but DevOps doesn’t replace a development process (planning, scope control, stakeholder validation). Scrum does.

### 8\) The other “process flows” (iterative / evolutionary / parallel)

These are *flows*, not full project governance models:

* Iterative: Scrum is iterative by design (each sprint).  
* Evolutionary: Scrum is also evolutionary (each sprint produces a working increment), which your PIR explicitly wants.  
* Parallel: Scrum allows parallel work *inside a sprint*, but forces integration into one increment, which prevents “5 separate parts that don’t connect.”

So Scrum dominates these by giving you the flow plus the team structure and control mechanism.

# 🖍️ Diagrams

# Diagrams

## UML use case diagram

### Student use cases

![][image1]

### Teacher use cases

![][image2]

### System us case

![][image3]

## [Student View Wireframe](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1ua4cg1d-XE7WcsWfow_syBqjcIkA5GMQ/view?usp=sharing)

### Login screen

The student experience begins before they see any game content. When a student accesses the platform, authentication is handled externally through CSN. The system receives a token that identifies the user and determines their role. If the user is recognized as a student, they are routed to the student interface. There is no visible login form; authentication is assumed to be handled transparently.

### Join Game Screen

![][image4]  
The first screen a student sees is the join screen. This page is intentionally minimal. It contains a single input field labeled “Enter class code” and a confirmation button such as “Join Game.” The purpose of this screen is to associate the authenticated student with a specific game instance. Each league is identified by a unique code provided by the professor. When the student enters the code, the system verifies that the game exists and that it is currently accepting participants. If the code is valid, the student waits for the professor to assign them to one of four teams configured for that league instance. If the code is invalid, an error message is displayed and the student remains on the same screen.

### Waiting screen

![][image5]  
When the student enters a valid code, the system waits for the professor to assign them to one of four teams configured for that league instance. While waiting the student sees a loading screen. Once the student is successfully assigned to a team, they are redirected to the main dashboard of the game.

### Main screen

![][image6]  
From this point forward, the interface maintains a consistent structural layout. At the top of the screen, a persistent header displays the identity and economic state of the team. On the left side of this header is the team name, clearly visible so that the student is constantly reminded which team they represent. On the right side are two dynamic indicators: the current fan count and the team’s available budget. These values change throughout the game as a result of match outcomes, marketing investments, and random events. Because fans generate revenue, these two indicators represent the core economic feedback loop of the simulation.

Below the header is a horizontal navigation bar containing five tabs: Team, Market, Marketing, Leaderboard, and Report. Selecting a tab replaces the central content area while the header remains fixed. This design reinforces that all decisions are made within the same ongoing league context.

#### Team tab

![][image7]  
The Team tab is the squad management screen. It visually resembles a sports management interface. The central area displays a stylized field layout where each position is occupied by a player card. Each card shows the player’s name, position, and overall rating. The layout reflects the structure of a rugby lineup and allows students to understand the composition of their team at a glance. Below the field is a list of substitute players and reserve players who are not currently in the starting lineup. Students can interact with this screen by selecting a position and replacing the current player with one from the bench. The chosen lineup determines the effective strength of the team in the next simulated match. Changes are saved when the player presses on the “ready” button. This screen emphasizes strategic optimization of performance based on available personnel.

#### Market tab

The Market tab represents the transfer and auction system. It displays a list or grid of players currently available for recruitment. Each entry shows the player’s name, position, overall rating, and the current highest bid. Students can submit bids directly on this screen by entering a value and confirming it. When a student submits a bid, the corresponding amount is temporarily reserved from the team’s budget. If another team outbids them, the reserved amount is automatically released back into their available funds. Bids cannot be manually withdrawn once placed. This creates a competitive, interactive environment in which teams must balance aggression in recruitment with financial sustainability. The market screen encourages risk assessment and strategic timing. The winner of the bid is decided when the decision phase of the cycle is finished meaning teams will only receive players at the result round and will be able to use the player on the next decision phase. Later we would like to add a search and filters feature for the market in order to make it easy for users to find a player that meets their budget constraints and their team needs.

#### Marketing

![][image8]  
The Marketing tab allows teams to influence their future financial growth. This screen presents a set of investment options such as advertising campaigns, merchandising expansion, or promotional initiatives. Each option displays its cost and a description of its expected impact. The effect is probabilistic rather than deterministic; investing in marketing increases the likelihood of gaining fans in the upcoming round. Because fans are the primary source of recurring revenue, marketing decisions affect long-term economic strength rather than immediate match performance. Students allocate a chosen amount of their budget to one or more marketing actions. Once confirmed, these investments are locked in for the round and factored into the simulation when the round concludes. 

### Leaderboard

![][image9]  
The Leaderboard tab provides an overview of the league’s competitive state. This screen resembles a professional tournament interface. It includes two primary sections: upcoming  matches, and the standings table. The matches section shows the fixtures for each round, indicating which teams are facing each other. The standings section displays all four teams along with their number of matches played, wins, losses, point differential, and total points. This screen allows students to contextualize their performance within the league and anticipate the importance of upcoming matches. It reinforces the competitive structure and progression over the six rounds.

#### Report

![][image10]  
The Report tab is the analytical component of the system. It presents historical data from previous rounds. Students can review past match results, revenue changes, fan growth, and other relevant statistics (fans gained/lost, money made, auctions won, players injured/sanctioned). This information supports strategic decision-making by helping teams identify trends, evaluate the effectiveness of prior marketing investments, and assess the impact of roster changes. The report view transforms the simulation from a simple game into a reflective management exercise, encouraging data-driven adjustments rather than reactive decisions.

## Teacher view wireframe

### Login screen

The teacher experience begins after authentication through CSN. Just like students, the teacher does not log in manually; their identity and role are determined from the token. Because their role is “teacher,” they are routed to the teacher dashboard rather than the student join screen.

### General management screen

![][image11]  
The first screen a teacher sees is a game management overview page. This page functions as a control hub rather than a game interface. At the top of the page there is a clear action button labeled something like “Create Game.” This button is the primary entry point for starting a new league instance. Below this button is a list of previously created games. Each entry in this list displays at least two essential pieces of information: the group or class name associated with the game, and the unique code that students must enter in order to join that instance.

When a teacher clicks “Create Game,” a new game instance is generated and immediately appears in the list below. The generated join code is displayed clearly so it can be shared with students. The teacher does not yet enter the game environment; they remain on the overview page until they select a specific game from the list. Each listed game acts as a navigational link. Clicking on a game takes the teacher into the management interface for that specific league. From that point onward, the teacher is operating within a particular game context. There is also a clear “Back” button that allows the teacher to return to the overview page and manage other game instances. This design makes it possible for a professor to run multiple leagues across different classes or semesters without confusion.

### Game management screen

![][image12]  
Once inside a specific game instance, the interface adopts a persistent structure similar to the student view. At the top left of the screen, the name of the game is displayed. This header remains fixed regardless of which management tab is currently selected. Beneath the header is a horizontal navigation bar containing five tabs: Teams, Parameters, Round Management, Report, and Leaderboard. Just like in the student interface, the header remains static and only the content below the tabs changes.

#### Teams tab

![][image13]  
The Teams tab is the first management tool available to the teacher. The screen is divided into two main areas. On the left side is a vertical list of all students who have joined the game using the provided code. This list shows every participant, regardless of whether they have been assigned to a team. On the right side of the screen are four clearly defined sections, each representing one of the four teams in the league. These sections function as containers.

The teacher can assign students to teams using a drag-and-drop interaction. A student’s name can be dragged from the left list and dropped into one of the four team containers. This interaction immediately associates the student with that team. Because all unassigned students remain visible in the left list, the teacher can quickly see who has not yet been placed. If necessary, a student can be moved from one team to another by dragging their name out of one container and into another. This interface makes team organization transparent and flexible, allowing the teacher to balance teams or reorganize assignments at any time before or during the game.

#### Parameters tab

![][image14]  
The Parameters tab gives the teacher control over the underlying mechanics of the simulation. This screen exposes adjustable sliders or numeric inputs that modify global game variables. Examples include the probability of player injury, the fan reward multiplier after a win, the financial growth factor, or other hidden parameters that influence how strongly decisions impact outcomes. These settings affect the upcoming round or possibly the entire league, depending on the implementation. By modifying these parameters, the teacher can adjust difficulty, emphasize certain strategic dimensions, or tailor the experience to pedagogical goals. The interface is designed to make these adjustments intuitive, likely using sliders rather than raw numeric inputs to avoid unnecessary complexity.

#### Round management tab

![][image15]  
The Round Management tab is the operational control center. This is where the teacher governs the temporal progression of the game. Instead of a single “Next Round” button, this screen provides explicit controls such as “Launch Round,” “Stop Round,” and “Move to Next Round.” When a round is launched, students are allowed to make decisions (adjust lineups, place bids, and invest in marketing). When the round is stopped, decision-making is locked and the simulation can be processed. The teacher then advances the game to the next round when appropriate.

This separation of actions is intentional. It prevents students from continuing to interact with the system outside of allocated class time. The teacher decides when decisions can be made and when they are finalized. On this same screen, there is also an indicator showing how many teams or students have marked themselves as ready. This allows the teacher to assess whether most participants have completed their decisions before closing the round. Rather than blindly progressing, the teacher has visibility into student engagement and can make informed timing decisions.

#### Report tab

![][image16]  
The Report tab in the teacher interface mirrors the report available to students, but it is expanded. Instead of seeing only the report for one team, the teacher can view reports for all four teams. The screen includes separate sub-tabs or selectable panels for each team. By switching between them, the teacher can analyze each team’s financial changes, fan growth, roster evolution, and match outcomes across all completed rounds. This consolidated oversight enables the teacher to observe strategic differences between teams and potentially use these differences as discussion material in class.

#### Leaderboard tab

![][image17]  
The Leaderboard tab in the teacher interface is visually identical to the student version. It displays match schedules and standings. The teacher does not see additional hidden ranking information; they see the same competitive state that students see. This symmetry reinforces transparency in competition while still preserving the teacher’s authority over progression and parameters.

## CRC cards for Rugby Team Management Game

# 📚 Stack

This stack uses TypeScript end-to-end. It is currently the industry standard, making it highly attractive for us students to learn, and its strict typing acts as built-in documentation for future teams.

* **Language:** **TypeScript**. This is non-negotiable for a handover project. It prevents massive classes of bugs and makes the data models (like Player, Team, Match) strictly defined and easy for future students to understand.  
* **Framework (The "Hybrid"):** **Next.js (React)**  
  * *Why:* allows you to mix Server-Rendered pages (fast, simple, great for the teacher's dashboard) with async API endpoints (for the live auction and decision-making UI) in the exact same repository.  
* **Database ORM:** **Prisma**.  
  * *Why:* Prisma provides a highly readable `schema.prisma` file that will allow future students to understand your entire database structure in exactly one glance. It handles PostgreSQL migrations beautifully.  
* **Styling:** **Tailwind CSS** \+ **shadcn/ui**.  
  * *Why:* You need a "modern, clean, minimalist" UI without spending weeks writing CSS. Shadcn gives you pre-built, accessible, clean components (buttons, tables for the league, dialogs) that you copy-paste and tweak.  
* **Authentication:** **Auth.js** (formerly NextAuth).  
  * *Why:* You need to integrate with INSA’s CAS module. These libraries have plugins or allow custom providers to handle standard SSO and CAS protocols smoothly.

### **Infrastructure & DevOps (INSA Environment)**

Because you are deploying to a provided virtual server, you must ensure the deployment process is reproducible for future teams.

* **Containerization:** **Docker & Docker Compose**. Do not install Node or Python directly on the server. Write a `Dockerfile` and a `docker-compose.yml` that spins up the app. This guarantees that if INSA wipes the server next year, the next team can run `docker compose up` and be back online in 2 minutes.  
* **CI/CD:** **GitLab CI** (since INSA provides GitLab Enseignement). Set up a simple pipeline that:  
  1. Runs the mandatory core engine tests.  
  2. Builds the Docker image.  
  3. Deploys to the INSA server (via SSH or GitLab Runner).  
* **Testing:** **Jest** (for TypeScript). As noted in our "Definition of Done," you only strictly need to test the game engine and state machine (e.g., ensuring points are calculated correctly and money cannot go below zero).

 It offers the best UI capabilities for the students and is highly relevant for web architecture learning.

Update him about the security

# 🏃 SPRINT 1

Sprint 1: Project Overview  
The primary focus of this sprint is setting up the core game entities, the "Teacher View" framework, and the "Team View" functionality.  
Roles and Responsibilities  
1\. Database (DB)  
• Assigned to: Abdel  
• Tasks: \* Overall database management and architecture.  
• Ensuring the "Model" (CRUD) connects correctly to the data store.  
2\. Game Instances  
• Assigned to: George & Thibaut  
• Tasks:  
• Create game entity: Implementing the logic to generate new game instances.  
• View entities: Creating the interface/logic to list existing games.  
• Rename entities: Allowing modifications to game instance names.  
• Generate game code: Developing the unique identifier system for games.  
3\. Teacher View Framework  
• Assigned to: Armand  
• Tasks:  
• Get game name: Fetching specific data points for the view.  
• Create tab: Developing the UI navigation/tabbing system.  
• Routing: Implementing the "Route back to game instance view" logic.  
4\. Team View  
• Assigned to: Daniel  
• Tasks:  
• Data Integration: Pull students from DB and populate the side list.  
• Management: Create team functionality.  
• UI/UX: Implement Drag and Drop features and the "Assigning Team" logic.  
5\. Templates (MVC Architecture)  
• Assigned to: Jenic  
• Tasks:  
• View: Building the frontend templates.  
• Controller: Setting up the Node.js routing (Route to game controller).  
• Model: Implementing the CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations

# Formulas

# **Game Model – Variables and Core Formulas (Improved Version)**

## **1\. Global Variables**

* `START_FAN_NUMBER = 1000`  
* `MAX_FAN_NUMBER = 1,000,000`  
* `START_TEAM_MONEY = 50,000`  
* `START_PUB_SCORE = 0`  
* `START_MERCH_SCORE = 0`  
* `INITIAL_PLAYER_STATS = 40` (all players start with this value)

---

## **2\. Player Market System**

At the beginning of each round, **15 new players** are generated with increasing skill levels:

players\_stats ∈ \[40 ; 40 \+ (round\_number × random(1,10))\]

### **Player Price (before auction)**

start\_price \= (players\_stats² / 10\) \+ (round\_number × 100\)

This quadratic scaling ensures that high-level players become significantly more expensive, preventing unrealistic accumulation of top players.

## **3\. Marketing Investments**

Each team can invest in:

* `pub_score`  
* `merch_score`

### **Constraints**

* Maximum score: `10` for each  
* Maximum investments: **2 per round per category**

### **Cost Function**

investment\_cost \= round\_number × 7000 × (1 \+ current\_score / 10\)

This introduces increasing marginal costs, limiting early-game overinvestment strategies.

## **4\. Match Outcome Calculation**

The performance of a team is computed using a weighted multi-factor model:

Team\_score \=  
(Avg\_players\_stats / 100\) × 0.4 \+  
(Fan\_number / MAX\_FAN\_NUMBER) × 0.2 \+  
((pub\_score \+ merch\_score) / 20\) × 0.2 \+  
luck × 0.2

### **Winning Rule**

* The team with the highest score wins  
* Scores are rounded to allow **draws**

## **5\. Points System**

* Win → `+3 points`  
* Draw → `+1 point`  
* Loss → `0 points`

## **6\. Fan Base Evolution**

Instead of exponential growth, fan evolution is controlled and incremental:

Fan\_gain \= Fan\_number × (0.05 \+ (pub\_score \+ merch\_score)/50)

bonus\_result \=  
\+5000 if win  
\+2000 if draw  
\-1000 if loss

Fan\_number \= min(MAX\_FAN\_NUMBER,  
                Fan\_number \+ Fan\_gain \+ bonus\_result)

This prevents both uncontrolled growth and sudden collapse.

## **7\. Financial System**

### **Revenues**

Revenue \= round\_number × 5000 × (Fan\_number / 5000\)

### **Match Bonus**

\+ round\_number × 10000 if win

### **Final Money Update**

Money \= Money \+ Revenue \+ Bonus

# Final presentation

A new serious game for INSA’s business classes : the management of a rugby team

**ABSTRACT :** 

This work presents the design and development of a serious game aimed at enhancing management education through an interactive and domain-specific simulation. Our project is motivated by the limitations of the existing business simulation game used in our school: “SIMGEST”. This game is still played during the business classes but the access is limited, onboarding is not smooth, and the tool is not designed for large cohorts where every student can connect, authenticate, and participate directly. To address this gap, we propose a rugby-based management game in which players control a team over a six-match tournament, making strategic decisions related to recruitment, marketing investments, and financial management. The system relies on a set of interconnected variables, including player performance, fan base evolution, and marketing impact, all combined through normalized and scalable formulas to ensure balanced gameplay and prevent dominant strategies. A key contribution of this work lies in the design of a simple yet extensible architecture, allowing future developers to easily introduce new rules and dynamics without restructuring the system. The game will include dedicated interfaces for both players and instructors, enabling real-time decision-making and pedagogical monitoring. Initial results show that the proposed model provides a coherent balance between strategic planning and engagement while maintaining educational value. This approach demonstrates the potential of domain-specific serious games to improve learning outcomes in management education. Future developments aim to transform this prototype into a full-scale business game deployable on INSA’s institutional infrastructure. 

**LITERATURE REVIEW (not sure of where we should put it):**

I.  Literature review

   
Serious games are digital games designed for purposes beyond entertainment such as learning, training or even behavior change. This can be applied in a lot of fields like education, information, marketing, politics or healthcare \[1\].  Numerous studies report positive effects of these games on the learner engagement, motivation and the learning outcomes. However, despite a lot of improvements during the last decades, serious games remain constrained by methodological limitations and a tension between pedagogical rigor and ludic engagement \[2\].

   
*A.*    *Learning effectiveness of serious games*

A lot of studies and reviews show that serious games can achieve learning outcomes comparable to, and sometimes better, than the traditional teaching methods. The popularity of videos games, especially among younger people, makes them an ideal medium for educational purposes \[3\]. To ensure the effectiveness of a serious game it always should be equipped with a game design rooted in the construct of gamification \[4\].

   
*B.*    *Application domains of serious games*

Serious games are applied across multiples domains with distinct objectives. In cultural heritage they can enhance historical learning through immersive virtual environments \[3\]. In healthcare and medical education they can support skill acquisition or clinical decision making \[2\]. They can also be used in education, ranging from elementary schools (with games for mathematics and foreign languages) to universities (with business and management games) \[5\]. This diversity of contexts implies domain-specific design and evaluation requirements rather than one-size-fits-all solutions.

   
*C.*   *Technical Approaches and Adaptation*

Technological innovation plays a central role in serious games development. New technologies like artificial intelligence or virtual reality can improve the immersive field. Adaptations techniques, like dynamic difficulty adjustment and intelligent non players characters, have been shown to improve  usability and engagement. However all these adaptations techniques still be used mainly in single player games since it is way more difficult to implement it in a multiplayer game \[6\].   .

 

.

*D.*   *Design and methodological challenges*

The literature consistently identifies challenges in integrating pedagogical objectives with engaging game mechanics : that’s why it can be hard to find an agreement between the game designer and the expert of the domain \[7\]. The serious  game should pretend to have a ludic aspect but it can’t always work when institutional rules constrain the user experience \[2\].

 

*E.* 	*Research gap and futures directions*

The main research gaps are the lack of standardized evaluation framework, the difficulty to find a balance between fun and learning \[2\] or the complex task of coding the random aspect of the real world in a game. There still also a lot of technical difficulties like adapting some tools of single player games to multiplayers one’s \[6\]. The scientific community recommand that future research should prioritize scientifically grounded design methodologies, rigorous experimental evaluations, and interdisciplinary collaboration. The improvement of the AI in the next years will also help a lot the serious game with more realistic non players character sfor example.

 

*Conclusion*

Overall, serious games represent a promising approach for enhancing engagement and learning across diverse domains. While current evidence supports their pedagogical value, methodological and design limitations continue to constrain their impact. Advancing the field requires stronger theoretical integration, improved evaluation practices, and scalable technical solutions, particularly for adaptive and multiplayer environments.

**INTRODUCTION:**

A we saw in the literature review, serious games are increasingly used in higher education to support active learning and decision-making skills, particularly in management and business-related courses. While existing studies confirm their pedagogical value in terms of engagement and learning outcomes, many current tools remain limited by rigid design, lack of scalability, and difficulties in adapting to modern teaching environments. These challenges are especially visible in multiplayer educational simulations, where balancing gameplay, pedagogy, and technical constraints remains complex.

At INSA, a business simulation game called Simgest is currently used to introduce third-year students to key management concepts such as financial analysis, decision-making, and performance evaluation. Although this tool provides a relevant educational framework, it suffers from several operational limitations: restricted accessibility, limited scalability for large student cohorts, and a lack of flexibility for future improvements. In addition, its proprietary nature prevents meaningful modifications, making it difficult to adapt to evolving pedagogical needs and technological standards.

To address these limitations, this project proposes the development of a new web-based serious game designed to replace and extend the existing system. The game is based on the management of a rugby team competing in a six-match tournament, providing a concrete and engaging context for applying management concepts. Players must make strategic decisions such as recruiting athletes, investing in marketing activities, and managing financial resources. These decisions directly influence the outcomes of matches, which are computed through a balanced model integrating player performance, fan base evolution, marketing impact, and controlled randomness.

A central aspect of the proposed system is its modular and scalable architecture. The game includes dedicated interfaces for players and instructors, supports authentication and multi-session gameplay, and is designed to be hosted on institutional servers. Moreover, the underlying model relies on normalized variables and interconnected formulas to ensure balanced gameplay while allowing future extensions, such as new rules or adaptive mechanisms, to be easily integrated by subsequent development teams.

The objective of this research is to design and evaluate a serious game capable of providing a more accessible, scalable, and engaging learning tool for management education. More specifically, this work investigates how a domain-specific simulation, combining economic, strategic, and stochastic elements, can improve both the usability and pedagogical effectiveness of multiplayer business games in an academic context.

**METHODS/ PRESENTATION OF THE GAME** 

**PT1 : Presentation of the hosting environments (work with the CSN, accessible by the insa’s login…)**  
**PT2 : Rapid presentation of how we coded the game : differents layers of code, scalability**

**PT3: Presentation of how the teacher crate a game** 

**PT4: Presentation of how a game works**

**PT5 : Presentation of how a round work (teacher view/Player view)**

**PT6 : Little presentation of how we implemented all the formulas ( based on the formulas page of this doc)**

**Results and discussion** 

Here we should talk about the game we tested : the results are balanced ? how it can help people to learn business ? how it can be upgraded by the followings team. 

### **Conclusion**

Summarize your key findings. Include important conclusions that can be drawn and further implications for the field. Discuss benefits or shortcomings of your work and suggest future areas for research.

You can recognize individuals who provided assistance with your work, but who do not meet the [definition of authorship](https://conferences.ieeeauthorcenter.ieee.org/author-ethics/ethical-requirements/#authorship). The acknowledgments section is optional.

### **References**

Provide citation information for all the previous publications referred to in your paper. Cite only those references that directly support your work.
